cot_prefix: Okay, let's try to figure out the answer here. The question is asking what we can learn from the passage. The story is about a farmer in central China who uses an old horse to plow his fields. When the horse dies, everyone feels bad, but the farmer just says "We'll see." Then, when the same thing happens with a new horse, and even when his son breaks his leg, he keeps saying the same thing. The twist comes when the army doesn't recruit his son because of the broken leg, which turns out to be a good thing. So, the main theme here seems to be about not jumping to conclusions about good or bad events because things might turn out differently later. The farmer remains calm and doesn't overreact, which the story's moral at the end reinforces. The question is asking which of the options (A to D) is correct based on the passage. Looking at the options: A) The farmer just wanted more help from other villages. Hmm, no, the villagers gave him a horse as a gift out of admiration, not because he asked for help. He didn't seem to be seeking more help. B) The farmer was well educated and knowledgeable. The story doesn't mention his education or knowledge directly. His calmness is more about his attitude towards events rather than education. C) The farmer's family was poor. Yes, the first paragraph says he didn't have money and used an old horse instead of a tractor. Also, when the villagers gave him a new horse as a gift, that suggests they might have thought he was in need, but the question is whether this is the correct answer. The question is about what we can learn from the passage, not just a fact stated in it. The moral is about not overreacting, so maybe option C is a fact but not the lesson. D) The farmer cared about anybody else but himself. The story doesn't really focus on his caring for others. He's more focused on maintaining his calm attitude. So D might not be correct.
